```


### Summary

[sample_rate](https://docs.rs/lofty/latest/src/lofty/properties/file_properties.rs.html#70-72) and [channels](https://docs.rs/lofty/latest/src/lofty/properties/file_properties.rs.html#80-82) returning `0` instead of `None` 

When used on a m4a file here are properties of said file:

```

```

### Expected behavior

It prints out None

Audio channels: None
Sample rate2: None
```

### Assets

_No response_

comment.body:
The zero comes from a placeholder leak. At `mp4/properties.rs:533` and `:542`, the generic `read_stsd` reads `channels` and `sample_rate` from the standard QuickTime sound entry slots before dispatching to a codec-specific parser. For `ec-3` (E-AC-3) those slots are placeholders — the real values live in the `dec3` sub-atom. The match at `:567` only dispatches `mp4a`/`alac`/`fLaC`/`drms`, so for `ec-3` the loop hits the `_` arm and `continue`s with `properties.channels == 0` and `properties.sample_rate == 0` still in place from the placeholder reads.

The leak survives the conversion because `Mp4Properties` stores `channels: u8` and `sample_rate: u32` (`:190`, `:192`) as plain values, no `Option`, while the public `FileProperties` has them as `Option<u32>` / `Option<u8>`. `From<Mp4Properties>` at `:203` / `:205` unconditionally wraps the bogus 0 in `Some(_)`.

Two fix shapes. Minimal: at `:203` and `:205`, guard the wrap on positive values — `if input.sample_rate > 0 { Some(input.sample_rate) } else { None }`, same for channels. Zero Hz / zero channels is never a valid audio configuration, so the sentinel is safe to interpret. Architectural: make `Mp4Properties.sample_rate` and `.channels` themselves `Option<_>` to match the public contract; all writes (`:533`, `:542`, `:799-:800`, `:804`, `:820`, `:829`, `:833`, `:885`, `:893`, `:928`, `:930`) wrap in `Some`, default is `None`. Both fix the reported case and any other codec the `:567` match doesn't dispatch today (`ec-3`, `opus`, `wave`, `dts`). Happy to PR either if you have a preference.

issue.title: fix(enumerations): walk MRO in EnumDefinitionMeta __contains__ / __getitem__
issue.body:
## Summary

Fixes #3591.

`EnumDefinitionMeta.__contains__` and `__getitem__` looked only at `cls.__dict__`, so subclasses inherited no permissible values from their parent enumeration class. `pythongen` emits empty wrapper subclasses for identifier slots whose range is an enum:

```python
class FIXDatatypeDatatypeName(FIXDatatypeName):
    pass
```

The wrapper inherits the parent's permissible values via the MRO, but the metaclass operators did not walk it, so every lookup against the wrapper raised:

```
ValueError: Unknown FIXDatatypeDatatypeName enumeration code: Country
```

This walks `cls.__mro__` in both operators. `__getitem__` now raises `KeyError` for missing keys, matching the implicit behaviour of the prior `cls.__dict__[item]` lookup; tests cover that path so the contract stays explicit.

## How was this tested?

`tests/linkml_runtime/test_utils/test_enumerations.py` (new) parametrises across a parent, an empty wrapper subclass (the reporter's shape), and a grandchild that defines its own value. It covers:

- `key in cls` finds inherited values; returns False for missing keys.
- `cls[key]` returns the nearest ancestor's `PermissibleValue`; raises `KeyError` for missing keys.
- `cls("inherited_code")` (the reporter's repro) succeeds on the wrapper and grandchild.

Stash-bisect confirms the new tests fail without the source change (6 fail / 12 pass) and pass with it (18/18). Full `tests/linkml_runtime/` suite: 1766 passed, 271 skipped, 2 xfailed locally — no regressions.

## Areas of uncertainty

- `EnumDefinitionMeta.__setitem__` is not touched. The duplicate-key guard there also only checks `cls.__dict__` (so it cannot detect inherited collisions), but `cls.__dict__[key] = value` already raises `TypeError: 'mappingproxy' object does not support item assignment`, so the write path itself is dead in the base class. Out of scope for this fix; happy to address in a follow-up if the maintainers would like the duplicate guard widened to the MRO and the assignment switched to `setattr(cls, key, value)`.

## Checklist

- [x] My code follows the [contributor guidelines](https://linkml.io/linkml/maintainers/contributing.html)
- [x] I have added tests that prove my fix/feature works
- [x] Existing tests pass locally with my changes

comment.body: For reference: the test failure is `ImportError: cannot import name 'uts46data' from 'idna.uts46data'`, which fires across other unrelated PR branches in the same window — e.g. docs/clarify-oocodegen-granularity at job 78641424366 fails identically on Python 3.13. Looks like an upstream idna packaging issue that landed after main last ran at d24624c (2026-05-29T03:29Z); every PR-branch CI run since 2026-05-29T16:11Z has hit the same import. Nothing in this diff touches idna or the rewrite-rules test — the change is scoped to `EnumDefinitionMeta.__contains__` and `__getitem__` walking the MRO.

issue.title: Setting OLLAMA_MODELS doesn't work correctly.
issue.body:
### What is the issue?

When setting the OLLAMA_MODELS variable to a custom path, e.g., /mnt/data/ollama/models 
as in the example below:

`
[Service]
Environment="OLLAMA_MODELS=/mnt/data/ollama/models"
`

The service reports errors and doesn't start correctly.
The very limited documentation doesn't mention any limitations or specifics of setting this variable.

In the example above, permissions are set for the `ollama/models` folders to `ollama:ollama` (user/group), but ollama doesn't have any write permissions to `/mnt/data`, which is correct and MUST be respected.

The expected behaviour is that the OLLAMA_MODELS path is used as provided without any question, other than whether it has read/write access to it.

`
`

### Relevant log output

```shell
Error: mkdir /mnt/data/ollama: permission denied: ensure path elements are traversable
```

### OS

Linux

### GPU

Nvidia

### CPU

Intel

### Ollama version

0.24.0

comment.body:
The error string `ensure path elements are traversable` is a hint from `manifest/paths.go:20`. When `os.MkdirAll` fails, the most common cause is the **execute (+x) bit** missing on a parent directory, distinct from write permission on the leaf.

Walking up from your error: `mkdir /mnt/data/ollama: permission denied` means the runtime can't read the existing `/mnt/data/ollama/models` through `/mnt/data`. `Stat` returns EACCES, `MkdirAll` interprets that as "doesn't exist" and tries to create the parent, which then fails with EACCES on `mkdir` for the same reason. Same shape as the closures in #10672, #9945, #4732.

Diagnostic command to print the mode of every directory in the chain:

```bash
p="/mnt/data/ollama/models"; ls -ld / $(while [ "$p" != "/" ]; do echo "$p"; p=$(dirname "$p"); done | tac)
```

If `/mnt/data` shows mode like `drwx------` or `drwxr-x---` and the ollama user is not the owner or in the owning group, the kernel won't let the ollama process traverse to reach the leaf. The fix is `chmod o+x /mnt/data` (or `chmod g+x /mnt/data` plus group membership), not a chmod on the leaf. The leaf is fine; the traversal is blocked.

The `+x` bit on a directory grants traversal, not modification. Read (`+r`) lets the user list entries, execute (`+x`) lets them open or stat entries by name. Granting `o+x` on `/mnt/data` is the standard pattern for shared parent paths and does not give ollama write access.

If you're on a distro where the ollama systemd unit has `ProtectHome=yes` or `ProtectSystem=strict`, those can also block writable access regardless of filesystem permissions. Check `systemctl cat ollama` for any `Protect*` directives near the top of the unit.
